Re: Recuegt for Formal Ailvice Resedine Potential Conllict of Interests
Dear Mr. Wallace:
I am writing to request formal advice pursuant to California Government
Cocle Section 8311(b) and 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18329(b)
regarcling my duties under the conllict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (Cal. Gov't. Code 0 81000 eL seq. arLd 2 CaI. Cocle Regs. $ 18700 er.
ceSJ. I am a Member of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board,
San trYancisco Bay Region ("Regional Board"). This letter contains all facts
material to my inqutry.
On the agenda before the Regional Board for the month of December 1992
ancl January 1993 is a Proposed Amenilment to the Water Qualiff Control Pian
setting effluent limitationg for selenium in San FYancisco Bay ("Proposed
Selenium Amendment'). I have encloseil, for your information, a draft copy of
the Proposed Selenium Amendment that will be presented to the Regional Board
in December and January, along with the staff report on the item.
My wife ov/ns, ali her separate property bought prior to our mariage,
common stock in Chevron U.S.A. valued at more than $1,000 but less than
$10,000. AII of the dividend income from my wife's investment is directly
reinvested in Chevron common sbock. I am informed that Chevron is a business
entity listed on the New York Stock Exchange and is also listed on the Fortune
Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U.S. industrial corporations.
The koposecl Selenium Amenilment is a revision to the San trYancisco
Region Basin Plan and, as such, is similar in nature to a general planning
document. If adopted, the Proposed Selenium Amenclment would apply to any
party <Iischarging effluent to San trYancisco Bay. Nevertheless, I am iaformed by
staff and legal counsel at the R€gional Board that the impact of the hoposed
Selenium Amendment will primarily affect oil refineries in the Bay Area in a


Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 2L, 1992
Page 2
manner that is ilistinguishable from the manner in which the clecision will a-ffect
the public generally, as set forth in Title 2 of California Code of Regulations
Section 18703 (hereinafter, "Regulations'). According to the stafr report for the
Proposed Selenium Amendment, oil refineries reportedly account for between 56 -
7O% of t}lre total eelenium discharged into San FYancisco Bay.
The Proposeil Selenium Amendment does not presently involve an
amenilment, revision or modification of Chevron'e existing permits issued by the
Regional Board, nor is Chevron a 'named party' in tJre proceecling in which the
hopoeed Selenium Amenilment wiII be hearil as contemplated in Regulations
Section 18?02.1(bX2). However, it is probable that, if the Proposed Selenium
Amendment is adopteil, Chevron's permits may need to be revised in the future.
I am informerl that it is not reasonably foreseeable at this time when Chewon's
permits may require revision if the hoposed Selenium Amenclment is adopted.
I have requested information from Chevron about potential economic
impacta to the company if the Proposed Selenium Amenilment is adopted-
According to the information provideil by Chewon in the attachecl letter from Mr.
Jerry Ross, the hoposed Selenium Amenilment, if adopted, would result in no
immediate increase or ilecrease to the gross revenues of Chevron in a frscal year
totalling $1,000,000 or more. Moreover, it does not appear reasonably foreseeable
that Chevron woulcl incur or avoitl aclditional expenses for a frscal year in the
amount of $250,000 or more. Depending on how the Regional Board chooses to
implement the hoposed Selenium Amenilment, it is possrble that Chevron may
incur expenses in excess of $250,000 or more in a fiscal year in order to comply
with future selenium ilischarge limitations. Nevertheless, Chevron maintains
that assessing the frnancial impacts of the Proposed Selenium Amendment in
future years is highly speculative.
I have three questions. First, based on my indirect frnancial interest in
Chevron and the economic effect of the Boposed Selenium Amendment on
Chevron as discuased above, do I have a "financial interest'in the hoposed
Selenium Amendment as contemplated under Government Code Section 87103
requiring my recusal on the matter? Second, is Chevron 'directly" involved in
the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated in Regulations Section
187O2.2b)? thiril, if my wife disposes of her investment in Chevron, by gift or
otherwise, to someone other than a member of my direct family, on what date
would my indirect frnancial interest in Chevron dissipate?
The Proposed Selenium Amendment is scheduled to be heard by the
Regional Board beginning on December 16, 1992 and will likely be continued to
the January 21, 1993 meeting. Therefore, I would greatly appreciat€ your
guiclance aa Boon as possible. I appreciate your attention to and assistance with


Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 21, 1992
Page 3
this matter. Prior to issuing your formal advice letter, I would appreciate the
opportunity to speak with you if you have any questions concerning the factual
backgrounil or other elements of this request,
CSIJ:bs
Enclosures
cc: Gary Grimm, Esq.
William Attwater, Esq.
Steve Rikhie
Very truly yours,

sections.question: First, based on my indirect financial interest in Chevron and the economic effect of the Proposed Selenium Amendment on Chevron as discussed above, do I have a "financial interest" in the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated under Government Code Section 87103 requiring my recusal on the matter? Second, is Chevron "directly" involved in the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated in Regulations Section 18702.2(b)? Third, if my wife disposes of her investment in Chevron, by gift or otherwise, to someone other than a member of my direct family, on what date would my indirect financial interest in Chevron dissipate?
sections.question_synthetic: A California Regional Water Quality Control Board member seeks advice on whether his wife's stock ownership in Chevron creates a disqualifying financial interest in a proposed amendment regulating selenium discharge that would primarily affect oil refineries including Chevron, and asks when such interest would terminate if his wife divests the stock.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or answer section is present. The document clearly contains three distinct questions from the requestor. Document appears complete with proper letterhead and signatures indicated.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: First, based on my indirect financial interest in Chevron and the economic effect of the Proposed Selenium Amendment on Chevron as discussed above, do I have a "financial interest" in the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated under Government Code Section 87103 requiring my recusal on the matter? Second, is Chevron "directly" involved in the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated in Regulations Section 18702.2(b)? Third, if my wife disposes of her investment in Chevron, by gift or otherwise, to someone other than a member of my direct family, on what date would my indirect financial interest in Chevron dissipate?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from a California Regional Water Quality Control Board member dated December 21, 1992, requesting formal FPPC advice regarding potential conflicts of interest arising from his wife's ownership of Chevron stock in connection with a proposed selenium discharge limitation amendment that would primarily affect oil refineries.
